graph.name: PixelStrings Architecture
graph.notes: PixelStrings is a service that processes video for retiming, deinterlacing, upscaling, noise removal, and retexturing. Users interact via a web browser to upload video clips to their own S3 buckets. A Job Manager orchestrates metadata extraction via Lambda, stores metadata in a database, and dispatches processing jobs to GPU-accelerated EC2 G3 instances. Processed files are returned to S3, with an option for IMF (Interoperable Master Format) wrapping. The platform scales to 200 concurrent jobs per region and 100 concurrent web users. It operates in US West, US East, and Ireland to minimize egress costs for users.

nodes.name: Web Browser
nodes.notes: End user interacts with the PixelStrings platform via a web browser (Chrome recommended) for login, account creation, S3 key input, workflow definition, job submission, and viewing metadata.

nodes.name: PixelStrings Server (Web Server / Job Manager)
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: This EC2 instance runs both the web server and the job manager. The web server handles user authentication, account management, and displays metadata/job status from the database. The job manager orchestrates the entire video processing workflow: it invokes Lambda for metadata extraction, saves metadata to the database, initiates GPU processing jobs, coordinates asset transfers (via an internal 'asset manager' function) between S3 and G3 instances, and manages IMF wrapping. The web server can handle about 100 users, and the job manager can handle up to 200 concurrent jobs per region. Currently, it's a single instance, with future plans for high availability. DATA_PEEK: Stores S3 keys entered by users for accessing their buckets.

nodes.name: Metadata & Job Database
nodes.notes: This database caches metadata extracted from video clips and stores account information, S3 keys, and job definitions. The web server retrieves metadata from this database to display to users, and the job manager stores processed metadata here. WORKLOAD_PEEK: High read/write for metadata caching and job state.

nodes.name: Customer's S3 Bucket
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Customers upload their input video clips to S3 buckets that they manage. Output processed video files are also written back to these buckets. The buckets are typically located in regions close to the GPU clusters (US West, US East, Ireland) to reduce egress costs.

nodes.name: Metadata Extractor
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Invoked by the Job Manager to get metadata from S3 input clips. These calls happen in the background, updating the UI every second or two. DATA_PEEK: Reads media and photo metadata from S3 objects.

nodes.name: GPU Processing Cluster (G3 Instances)
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: A cluster of GPU-enabled EC2 G3 instances performs the core video processing tasks, including retiming (Wormhole), de-interlacing (Spacetime), noise removal, and retexturing (Dark Energy). These are GPU-accelerated. Up to 200 jobs can run in parallel per region. If a GPU instance fails, the Job Manager detects a timeout and replaces it, ensuring job continuity. DATA_PEEK: Processes large video files; output is written to local disk before being transferred back to S3 or to the IMF service.

nodes.name: IMF Wrapping Service
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: This service performs Interoperable Master Format (IMF) wrapping for processed video. It's used when a user selects IMF as the output format in their workflow. The processed data is sent here from the G3 cluster before the final output is copied to the customer's S3 bucket. DATA_PEEK: Takes processed video data and adds IMF metadata and structure.

edges.notes: User accesses the web server for login, account creation, and entering S3 bucket keys. Also used for defining processing workflows and submitting jobs.
Web server stores account information, user-provided S3 keys, and job definitions in the database. Job Manager saves extracted metadata to the database.
Web server retrieves metadata and job status from the database to display in the user interface.
User directly uploads their input video clips to their own S3 bucket.
Job Manager invokes the Lambda function to extract metadata from clips in the customer's S3 bucket.
Lambda reads metadata from the video clips stored in the S3 bucket.
S3 returns metadata to the Lambda function.
Lambda returns extracted metadata to the Job Manager.

Job Manager submits a processing job to the GPU cluster, instructing it on the required workflow (retiming, deinterlacing, etc.). The Job Manager orchestrates the 'asset manager' function to copy input clips to G3 local disk.
Input video clips are copied from the customer's S3 bucket to the local disk of the G3 processing instances, orchestrated by the Job Manager's 'asset manager' function.
If the workflow specifies IMF output, the processed video data from the G3 cluster is sent to the IMF Wrapping Service.
After IMF wrapping, the final IMF output is copied back to the customer's S3 bucket. This transfer is orchestrated by the Job Manager's 'asset manager' function after IMF wrapping completes.
If IMF output is not required, the processed output video clips are copied from the G3 local disk back to the customer's S3 bucket, orchestrated by the Job Manager's 'asset manager' function.
